narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 51% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile of 4—below the typical student's 5.71—and your Year 3 decile of 1, which is much lower than the average of 5.53. Your LSAT score of 34 also falls short of the dataset average of 36.7, and your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 is slightly under the usual 3.21. While your upper-middle family income is solid, your status as a full-time student and your demographic profile as a white female align closely with the majority of applicants—full-time makes up 93% and white students 84%—so they didn't sway things much either way. The factors that most strongly influenced the model's push toward predicting a bar failure, in order of their impact, were your Year 1 decile, followed by your LSAT score, Year 3 decile, family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, full-time status, and gender. Each of these tilted the prediction slightly toward failure rather than success, with your earlier and later school deciles and LSAT standing out as the biggest drivers because they were notably below what the model sees in students who typically pass. Compared to peers who pass the bar, your grades and test scores are in the lower range of the distribution, making the model cautious about your odds. That said, this isn't a definitive judgment on your potential—it's just one data-driven view. To boost your chances next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above 37, strengthen your GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and build a track record of academic improvement that could lift those decile rankings in future evaluations. Many students in similar spots have turned things around by targeting test prep and targeted study habits. We encourage you to reapply after addressing these areas, as even small gains there could flip the prediction in your favor. Keep pushing—you've got this.
